html:
Astrid Sofia Lovisa Thyra, born November 17, 1905, at the Crown Prince's Palace in Stockholm, and died August 29, 1935, in Küssnacht, was a Princess of Sweden, Duchess of Brabant from 1926 to 1934, and Queen of Belgium from 1934 to 1935.

Astrid was the youngest daughter of Prince Carl and granddaughter of Oscar II. She married Prince Leopold of Belgium in 1926, and they had three children together. She died in a car accident in August 1935; King Leopold was driving and survived with minor injuries.

== Childhood ==

Princess Astrid was born on November 17, 1905, at the Crown Prince's Palace located at Gustav Adolfs torg in Stockholm. She was Prince Carl's third daughter, after Märtha and Margaretha. Along with her sisters and younger brother Carl, Astrid grew up on Djurgården, in a villa known as Byströms Villa, later also called Prince Carl's Palace. They lived there until 1923, when the family moved due to financial reasons. The summers were spent at Villa Fridhem, located on the northern shore of Bråviken. During her childhood, Astrid learned French, how to play the piano, and ballet. After her religious confirmation in 1923, she took practical courses, including sewing. After New Year's Day in 1925, Astrid began a four-month course in childcare.

== Marriage ==

In March 1926, the Belgian Queen Elisabeth made an anonymous visit to Stockholm together with her son, Crown Prince Leopold. During a visit to Elisabeth's friend, Ingeborg (Astrid's mother), Astrid had her first meeting with Leopold. They met again at a christening in Paris, and King Albert of Belgium invited Princess Ingeborg along with her daughters Märtha and Astrid to stay for a few days at one of the king's summer residences. That same summer, Leopold was also invited to visit Sweden again and stayed for several weeks at Fridhem. Leopold remained incognito during his time in Sweden, being presented as a Belgian lieutenant on a study trip. However, the secret eventually came out, and it became clear that there was a romance between Crown Prince Leopold of Belgium and Princess Astrid. On September 21, 1926, their engagement was officially announced.

The civil wedding ceremony took place on November 4th in the Rikssalen (National Hall) at Stockholm Castle. This was the first civil ceremony ever held in the castle, officiated by the Mayor of Stockholm, Carl Lindhagen. After the wedding dinner that evening, the Belgian royal couple and the Crown Prince left Stockholm and returned to Belgium to prepare for the religious wedding ceremony in Brussels. Princess Astrid traveled from Malmö aboard the Swedish cruiser HMS Fylgia to Antwerp, where she was greeted by thousands of waiting people. On November 10th, the couple was married in the cathedral of Sainte Gudule in Brussels, officiated by Archbishop van Roey.

== Duchess of Brabant ==

After settling in at Stuyvenberg Castle in Laeken, Brussels, the couple also had access to a villa in the seaside resort of Le Zoute, a hunting lodge in Ciergnon, and a summer house in Ostend. After Christmas in 1926, the couple embarked on an extensive tour of Belgium so that Astrid and the Belgian people could get to know each other better. In December 1928, the royal couple traveled to Ceylon, Singapore, Java, and New Guinea, returning to Belgium only in May 1929. In 1932, the couple made a three-month visit to Belgian Congo.

In October 1927, their first child, Joséphine-Charlotte, was born. Baudouin was born in the autumn of 1930, and Albert in the summer of 1934. Every summer, she visited Fridhem in Sweden with her children for a couple of weeks.

== Queen ==

Following King Albert's sudden death in February 1934, Leopold became King of Belgium, and Astrid became Queen. On February 23rd, Leopold took his oath at the Palais Législatif in Brussels, pledging to uphold the constitution. As Queen, Astrid assumed additional representative duties. Astrid initiated the formation of the "Comité National des Secours," which organized a fundraising campaign to help the poor and those in need. Astrid personally donated half a million francs, and the organization collected a total of 10 million francs.

== Death == On August 29, 1935, the couple was in Switzerland for an excursion in the Alps. After leaving their villa, they were driving towards Küssnacht on Lake Vierwaldstättersee. The King was driving, and Astrid was sitting in the front passenger seat next to him with a map on her lap. While the King glanced quickly at the map, the car skidded, veered off the road, and crashed into a tree. Astrid was thrown from the car and struck the tree, dying instantly. The funeral ceremony took place on September 3rd at the Saints-Michel-et-Gudule Cathedral in Brussels. Following the ceremony, she was buried in the Church of Our Lady in Laeken.

== Honors == The asteroid 1128 Astrid is named after her.

== Ancestry ==

== See also == The author and priest Martin Liljeblad – regarding controversies surrounding spiritist books and photographs.

== References ==

=== Notes ===

=== Printed Sources === "The Book about Astrid." The Swedish princess who became the Queen of Belgium. Published by Husmodern, 1935. Svenska Dagbladet Yearbook: 1926, edited by Erik Rudberg, Stockholm, 1927, pp. 86ff. Sparre, Anna (1985). "My Friend." Stockholm: AWE/Geber. ISBN [ISBN number].

How certain are we about this?

Information about people from the 20th century is extremely well-documented. Uncertainty rarely concerns factual information but rather interpretation and evaluation, which can change over time and depending on the observer.
tldr: Princess Astrid was a Swedish princess who became Queen of Belgium after marrying the Belgian Crown Prince. She tragically died in a car accident in 1935 at a young age.
lattlast:
Astrid was born in Stockholm in 1905 and grew up as a princess. During her childhood, she learned French, piano, and dancing, among other things. In 1926, she married the Belgian Crown Prince Leopold, and they had three children together.

After the Belgian King died in 1934, Astrid became Queen of Belgium. As queen, she worked extensively to help poor people and raised large sums of money for charitable causes. She was highly regarded throughout Belgium.

In 1935, Astrid died in a car accident in Switzerland while on an outing with her husband. He survived the accident but sustained minor injuries. After her death, a large funeral was held in Belgium, and an asteroid has been named after her.
